author: @ancapftw9113
text:
I was actually thinking of the idea of using a solar cell tuned to heat instead of visible light to convert energy from heat sources, including radioactive materials, industrial sources, and geothermal.

Combining this with your transparent graphene, transparent solar cell, and other transparent materials could help in a lot of areas.  You could use it to absorb IR from the sun to lower the temp of inner planets or low the heating of inner system probes.  You might even make nuclear reactors more efficient if you can get this to work at temps of a few hundred degrees C.

I'll have to add this to my list of things to research.

author: @garybarbourii8274
text: be cautious Robert, I have heard that the laws in regards to priority of record for patents changed in 2012 and no longer apply. there's a few things I'd like to patent here in America, but I'm not sure if that would also apply in the UK

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ah mate - the patent system is in utter disarray - there is no need to be careful the thing is such a mess it is quickly becoming unenforcable - did you know apple where awarded a patent for the rectangle and amazon hold the patent for the white backdrop - the chase for patents is hanging itself. Personally if someone sends me a cease and desist i just ignore it - nothing happens - mostly the guys sending such things are just trying to shake industry down - they used to do something similar here with stationary invoices.

replies.author: @garybarbourii8274
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith right on! I just plan on selling whatever I build without all the paperwork

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good on you mate -plus - of course it's actually relatively trivial to get round a patent - especially as the patent tells you what to do and the patent references tell you what they ripped off and who ripped them off too - have a look at the patents referenced in and the patents that reference the one someone says you are using

author: @jgoldbla
text: Robert, great posts I admire your spirit and ingenuity you are truly the real deal.  One note, the fact that there are alphanumeric numbers in the patent identifier means that the patent is not granted, but rather only an application.  I think that it likely that broad claims like these are most often the first items to be disputed by the USPTO.  On that same topic , in my opinion it is a sad state of affairs when the mind set of educational institutions think that it is wise patenting any discoveries, as it will ultimately lead to the hoarding of knowledge rather than the sharing of knowledge, and end up costing them future discoveries in the end.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +jgoldbla Thanks for the info mate and i absolutely agree with your view point on the hoarding of knowledge - i can understand why it is sometimes necessary - but to do it automatically is sad - cheers mate

author: @TheGEODEAHOLIC
text: as I understand it, #1 once something has been released into the public domain it may not be patented, #2 patents may only cover the particular & specific method or way they exploit a given natural principle; if they attempt to patent the principle itself they are invalid & may be defeated, though that'd need a lawyer, & lawyers generally rank purty high on the LP (loathesome parasite) scale...

author: @K9JT
text:
I think it's shameful for ANY university to patent anything. Just on principle alone and what the education system SHOULD stand for. There should be an international law against that kind of evil. To add to the absurdity, they try to patent methods that they themselves haven't even invented/ invented yet. There's a word for that...I think it's called... STEALING!
Even though it hasn't been granted yet, and that it may be narrowed down before it gets approved, it's still a diabolical strategy. I think they care more about the publicity and the research grants than the actual technology. Also, just having proof that they applied for the patent, discourages a whole lot of other less-informed people who could otherwise do a lot more work than they could/would.
On a slightly side note... interesting to know just how many "patent-pending" products end up being "successfully patented" products.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It's very small - about one in 5,000 patents make it to a useful product - that's not exact but near enough - and I absolutely agree with you

replies.author: @deangermeten5629
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
Robert: I once spoke to a defense contractor on the phone who told me the only way to commercialize an idea is to "build a fence around it." What he was talking about is IP protection, and the notion of commercialization is to put a product on the market that people will actually buy. The alternative is Open Source but the problem with that is investors won't touch it and no one will develop it to the point of commercialization because it allows for unlimited competition. So very many good ideas never see the light of day because most OS developers don't have enough funds to commercialize their own ideas. Of course canned goods do sell because people like to eat, so I don't know why the same principle can't work for other products as well, but it just doesn't. Have you seem Michelle Dubois' concept of copyright protection of ideas? (www.sosinventions.ca)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
dean germeten
I tried the link but it didn't work for me - i'll look into it later. I don't have a problem with IP protection per se - I have a problem with the abuse of IP and the ad hoc way it is applied

author: @MYNICEEV
text: MMMM. So one of the things that come to mind for the Thermocell. Is if NASA wanted to develope a power system for Astronauts where threir body heat and solar heat from the sun supplimented the battery power packs they would carry on EVA. By using the Thermocell based on your design making the back packs lighter. Thereby allowing the Astronaut more freedom of movement. Princeton Universities lawyers would have a field day. Your radiator with modification could make a heated chair for the elderly who could not afford to pay for heating. Would stop them becoming hypothermic and dieing. Princeton University lawyers would have a field day. If for example they stopped elderly peoples radiator chairs from being produced and it WAS proved their action killed many elderly people. Could they be done for culpable homizide? A very interesting thought. Because that should include the lawers that deffended them too. Because by deffending them the lawyers could possibly be done for aiding Princeton University in homicide. THIS IS MY OPINION ONLY! People don't lets turn this into an argument on Roberts video. What Princeton University has done is stupid. It stops competition dead.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it touches on a sore spot mate - people are going to get upset - even so , we should lose sight of what we do here

replies.author: @MYNICEEV
replies.text: We could never lose sight of what has and will be achieved by the open source community. To go after us small fry individualy around the world would cost them more money than it would be worth. They could take all my assets (£0.00). They could throw me in jail. They may as well throw the key away. As soon as I got out I would be at it again. Imagine the cost to them if the world was found to harbour 1000+ Raymond Earle's. Unable to get any money out of us due to zero assets.They may be able to stop people with assets. But they cannot stop those people talking to people like me. If we choose to impliment things from conversations. It's nothing to do with the person with assets. I chose to impliment the idea I wasn't told to. WHO pays the lawyers. MMM. Guess it's Princeton University then. Shame. Face bothered? Nah! Chin up my friend. Enjoy the sun.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: power of the community mate

replies.author: @MYNICEEV
replies.text: Too true my friend.
